questions.question: How many services does the IEEE 802.11 standard define, and into how many groups are they divided?
options: 6 services, split into 2 groups: station and distribution
9 services, split into 2 groups: station and distribution
12 services, split into 3 groups: station, distribution, and spectrum
4 services, all station-local
questions.explanation: IEEE 802.11 defines exactly 9 services: 5 distribution services (Association, Reassociation, Disassociation, Distribution, Integration) that involve the DS, and 4 station services (Authentication, Deauthentication, Privacy, MSDU delivery) that are local to a single STA.

questions.question: What is the primary security weakness of WPA2-Personal (PSK) mode that WPA3/SAE is designed to eliminate?
options: The GTK is transmitted in plaintext during the group-key handshake
AES-128-CCM can be brute-forced without capturing any handshake
A passive attacker who captures the 4-way handshake can mount an offline dictionary attack against the PMK
The Association ID (AID) leaks the passphrase to any nearby station
questions.explanation: With WPA2-Personal, PMK = PBKDF2(password, SSID, 4096, 256). A passive attacker who records the 4-way handshake can loop through password guesses, recompute PBKDF2, replay the handshake messages, and check whether the resulting MIC matches — with no limit on guess rate. WPA3/SAE uses Dragonfly so this offline replay is impossible.

questions.question: In the WPA2 EAPOL 4-way handshake, which message is the first to carry a valid MIC, and who sends it?
options: Message 1 — sent by the AP after generating the ANonce
Message 2 — sent by the STA after deriving the PTK
Message 3 — sent by the AP after verifying the STA knows the PMK
Message 4 — sent by the STA as a final acknowledgment
questions.explanation: Message 1 (AP → STA) carries the ANonce but has MIC=0 and Install=0 because the PTK has not yet been derived. Message 2 (STA → AP) is the first frame to carry a valid MIC: the STA derives the PTK from PMK + ANonce + SNonce + MACs and computes HMAC over the SNonce payload using the KCK portion of the PTK.

questions.question: The 384-bit PTK is split into three 128-bit sub-keys: KCK, KEK, and TK. Which sub-key wraps the GTK inside EAPOL message 3?
options: KCK (Key Confirmation Key) — it computes the MIC on all EAPOL messages
KEK (Key Encryption Key) — it encrypts the GTK in message 3
TK (Temporal Key) — it feeds AES-CCM for both data and management frames
The GTK is not encrypted in message 3; it is sent in plaintext
questions.explanation: PTK = KCK (bits 0–127) || KEK (bits 128–255) || TK (bits 256–383). The KEK is used specifically to wrap (encrypt) the GTK in message 3 using RFC 3394 AES Key Wrap. The KCK computes the MIC on messages 2, 3, and 4. The TK feeds the AES-128-CCM cipher for actual data-frame encryption.

questions.question: During a WPA2 BSS handoff (reassociation), which frame type explicitly carries the 'Current AP Address' field so the new AP can forward buffered frames?
options: Authentication Request
Association Request
Reassociation Request
Probe Request
questions.explanation: The Reassociation Request frame (frame type 0, subtype 2) carries the Current AP Address field. The new AP uses this to contact the old AP via the DS, retrieve any buffered frames, and update DS forwarding tables. Association Request does not carry this field; it is only used for the initial join.

questions.question: Which 802.11 amendment reduces roaming latency by pre-establishing PMK-R0 and PMK-R1 key material before the handoff occurs?
options: 802.11k — Radio Resource Measurement, which provides neighbor reports
802.11r — Fast BSS Transition, which pre-derives per-AP PTK material
802.11v — BSS Transition Management, which directs the STA to a new AP
802.11i — the original WPA2 amendment that added AES-CCM
questions.explanation: 802.11r (Fast BSS Transition) pre-establishes a PMK-R0 at the home AAA and derives a PMK-R1 for each candidate AP before the station roams. At handoff the PTK can be computed locally from cached key material, collapsing the 4-way handshake to 2 messages or eliminating it entirely in FT-over-DS, cutting total handoff latency to single-digit milliseconds.
